chunk_summary: This section discusses interpreting weight, length, and head circumference patterns in faltering growth, emphasizing the use of growth standards, Z-scores, and targeted testing to identify underlying causes and guide management strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of tracking weight, length/height, and head circumference trends in faltering growth.
Learn to interpret discordant growth patterns and their potential underlying causes.
Recognize when to use targeted testing versus extensive laboratory panels in faltering growth cases.
key_facts: WHO defines severe acute malnutrition as weight-for-length/height below -3 Z, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral nutritional edema.
Weight faltering with preserved length/height often indicates inadequate intake or feeding interaction issues.
Weight and length/height faltering together suggest chronic systemic disease, malabsorption, or increased metabolic demand.
Head circumference deceleration, especially with developmental concerns, raises risks for neurologic or genetic conditions.
Writing growth interpretations as a narrative reduces over-testing and focuses clinical decision-making.
reference_quote: WHO classification defines severe acute malnutrition using weight-for-length/height below -3 Z, MUAC below 115 mm, or bilateral nutritional edema.
document_key_topics: Interpreting growth patterns in faltering growth
Use of Z-scores and growth standards
Identifying underlying causes of growth faltering
